coredata.dc:title: Various carbon-based MgAl2O4 adsorbents and their removal efficiency of CR dye and antibiotics in aqueous media: High selective adsorption capacity, performance prediction and mechanism insight

coredata.dc:description: Various carbon -based MgAl2O4 (MAO) adsorbents were synthesized by a simple ultrasound irradiation technology with the activated carbon (AC), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), C3N4 (CN) and graphene oxide (GO) as carbon sources. The optimum synthesis conditions for the carbon -based MAO adsorbents were determined by the study of the addition of different types of carbon and different mass ratios of mVarious carbon: mMAO = 2.5%, 5.0%, 7.5% and 10%. The carbon -based MAO adsorbents were used for the adsorption of Congo red (CR) dye, tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OCH), ciprofloxacin (CIP), naproxen sodium (NPS) and tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA) and the seven factors affecting the adsorption behavior were discussed, including the type of carbon, AC content, initial dye concentration (CCR), adsorbent dosage (Ccarbon -based MAO adsorbents), adsorption time (At), pH value and reaction temperature (T). The corresponding adsorption isotherm models such as Langmuir, Tempkin, Koble-Corrigan, Toth and Freundlich models and thermodynamics of carbon -based MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of CR dye were also studied. When pH = 7.8, CCR = 200 mg/L, At = 90 min, T = 287 K and CAC 5.0 wt%-MAO = 1 g/L, the removal rate of CR dye reaches 89.7%. The back propagation (BP) neural network model was used to predict the adsorption percentage of carbon-based MAO adsorbents, which was consistent with the experimental results. The high adsorption capacity of AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of CR dye can be assigned to the synergistic effects of electrostatic interaction, intermolecular force, n-π interaction and hydrogen bonding, while for the adsorption of TCH, OCH and CIP can be ascribed to the electrostatic interaction. This study demonstrated a huge potential of carbon -based MAO adsorbents as an eco-friendly adsorbent for the adsorption of dyes and drugs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Various carbon -based MgAl2O4 (MAO) adsorbents were synthesized by a simple ultrasound irradiation technology with the activated carbon (AC), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), C3N4 (CN) and graphene oxide (GO) as carbon sources. The optimum synthesis conditions for the carbon -based MAO adsorbents were determined by the study of the addition of different types of carbon and different mass ratios of mVarious carbon: mMAO = 2.5%, 5.0%, 7.5% and 10%. The carbon -based MAO adsorbents were used for the adsorption of Congo red (CR) dye, tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OCH), ciprofloxacin (CIP), naproxen sodium (NPS) and tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA) and the seven factors affecting the adsorption behavior were discussed, including the type of carbon, AC content, initial dye concentration (CCR), adsorbent dosage (Ccarbon -based MAO adsorbents), adsorption time (At), pH value and reaction temperature (T). The corresponding adsorption isotherm models such as Langmuir, Tempkin, Koble-Corrigan, Toth and Freundlich models and thermodynamics of carbon -based MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of CR dye were also studied. When pH = 7.8, CCR = 200 mg/L, At = 90 min, T = 287 K and CAC 5.0 wt%-MAO = 1 g/L, the removal rate of CR dye reaches 89.7%. The back propagation (BP) neural network model was used to predict the adsorption percentage of carbon-based MAO adsorbents, which was consistent with the experimental results. The high adsorption capacity of AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of CR dye can be assigned to the synergistic effects of electrostatic interaction, intermolecular force, n-π interaction and hydrogen bonding, while for the adsorption of TCH, OCH and CIP can be ascribed to the electrostatic interaction. This study demonstrated a huge potential of carbon -based MAO adsorbents as an eco-friendly adsorbent for the adsorption of dyes and drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Dyes, drugs and persistent organic pollutants (POPs) are three important substances that seriously affect the quality of human production and life [1–6]. Among the three substances, dyes and POPs are recognized organic pollutants, but drugs are usually considered as good medicines to cure many biological diseases such as human beings, animals and aquatic products, so it is difficult to be associated with pollutants [7–11]. In particular, antibiotics are a class of drugs that can only be partially absorbed in the body of organisms, and the rest will be discharged into the environment with feces, causing great pollution to the environments [12–14]. Therefore, countries all over the world are looking for effective technical means to remove residual dyes, drugs and POPs from the water environment and soil to reduce their pollution to the environment.

To remove the environmental pollution caused by dyes, drugs and POPs, various advanced technologies have been developed to treat them and reduce their harm to the environment, including photocatalysis, thermal catalysis, ultrasonic catalysis, electrocatalysis, piezoelectric catalysis, adsorption, membrane separation and biodegradation [15–22]. Among these technologies, adsorption is an effective way to remove dyes, drugs and POPs without external energy [23]. The key to achieve high adsorption capacity for the removal of dyes, drugs and POPs is to select high efficient adsorbents [24]. Recently, the MgAl2O4 (MAO) absorbents have been used to remove the dyes, drugs and POPs due to its special crystal structure, electronic structure and surface charge state [25]. However, the adsorption capacity of MAO adsorbent for the removal of dyes, drugs and POPs is poor, so special technical means are urgently needed to improve its adsorption capacity.

Carbon-based materials including activated carbon (AC), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nitride (C3N4, CN) and graphene oxide (GO) have been widely studied for the adsorption of dyes, drugs and POPs due to their high physical and chemical stability, thermal stability and high adsorption capacity [26–29]. The combination of carbon-based materials and MAO in a special way are expected to improve the adsorption capacity of carbon-based MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of dyes, drugs and POPs. A simple ultrasonic irradiation technology has performed well in the synthesis of carbon-based MAO adsorbents, and is expected to be use the technology to synthesize carbon-based MAO adsorbents and improve its adsorption capacity. Therefore, it is of great significance to synthesize various carbon-based MAO adsorbents by a simple ultrasonic irradiation technology and compare their adsorption capacities for the adsorption of dyes, drugs and POPs.

To develop a new adsorbent that has good removal efficiency for the adsorption of dyes, drugs and POPs, in this paper, various carbon -based MgAl2O4 (MAO) adsorbents were synthesized by a simple ultrasound irradiation technology with AC, CQDs, CN and GO as carbon sources, which has not been reported for the adsorption of dyes, drugs and POPs. This study combined X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to characterize the phase structure, purity, charge state and composition of carbon -based MAO adsorbents, taking into account the heterogeneity, interface structure and interaction between different type of carbon and MAO. Meanwhile, Congo red (CR), (tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OCH), ciprofloxacin (CIP), naproxen sodium (NPS)) and tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA) were selected as the representatives of dyes, drugs and POPs to perform the adsorption experiments, respectively. To reveal the adsorption mechanism of carbon -based MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of dyes, drugs and POPs, the adsorption percentage, adsorption isotherm and thermodynamics were discussed on the basis of theoretical models, spectral data and adsorption experiments. This work provided clear explanations and experimental ideas for exploring the adsorption behavior and adsorption mechanism of carbon -based MAO adsorbents, and guidance for using multiple characterization data and theoretical models to clarify the dependent behavior of phase structure, microstructural and adsorption process.

Aluminium sulfate octadecahydrate (Al2(SO4)3·18H2O, 99.99%) and basic magnesium carbonate ((MgCO3)4·Mg(OH)2·5H2O, 99.99%) were purchased from Chengdu Aikeda Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Analytical grades of glucose (GL), acrylamide (AM), citric acid (CA), N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBAM), CR and AC were purchased from Tianjin Kemio Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. CQDs and CN were made in our laboratory. The melamine and ammonium chloride were purchased from Shandong Yusuo Chemical Technology Co., LTD. GO was purchased from Kaina Carbon New Materials Co., LTD. Tetracycline hydrochloride, oxytetracycline hydrochloride and ciprofloxacin hydrochloride were purchased from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. The above reagents are applied to experiments without further purification.

MAO nanoparticles were successfully synthesized by a facile polyacrylamide gel method [30,31]. According to the stoichiometric ratio of MgAl2O4 (nMg:nAl = 1:2), appropriate amounts of Al2(SO4)3·9H2O and Mg(CH3COO)2·4H2O were weighed. Al2(SO4)3·9H2O and Mg(CH3COO)2·4H2O are successively added into 20 mL deionized water, and the final concentration of metal ions (MI) is 0.015 mol/L. Subsequently, 4.7282 g CA, 20 g GL, 9.5958 g AM and 1.9192 g MBAM were weighed and added to above solution. The molar ratios of nCA:nMI, nAM:nMI and mass ratio of mAM:mMBAM were 1.5:1, 9:1 and 5:1, respectively. CA, GL and MBAM were used as chelating agents, gel collapse prevention agents and crosslinking agents, respectively. After the above reagents were completely dissolved, the jelly like gel was obtained by heating up to 90 °C for 1–5 h. Each step above was accompanied by magnetic agitation. The jelly-like gel was transferred to a drying oven at 120 °C for 24 h to obtain black xerogel. The black xerogel is then ground into powder and placed in a corundum crucible, and finally put into the tube sintering furnace and sintered at 800 °C for 5 h to obtain the MAO nanoparticles.

Mixed 1 g melamine and 4 g ammonium chloride evenly and grind in a mortar for 1 h. The powder was transferred to the porcelain boat, which was placed in a tube sintering furnace and sintered at 520 °C for 4 h. After the tube sintering furnace is cooled to room temperature, the products are collected and ground to obtain CN.

CQDs were prepared by using GL as the carbon source [32]. 10 g GL was dissolved in 800 mL deionized water and completely dissolved by ultrasound for 30 min and magnetic stirring for 1 h. The solution was then transferred to teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (100 mL) and reacted at 180 °C for 4 h. After the reaction is completed and cooled to room temperature, the above solution is filtered by millipore filter paper two times to obtain the suspension of CQDs.

Firstly, a certain mass or volume percentage of AC, CN, CQDs or GO was weighed or measured with measuring cylinder and added into 100 mL beakers. Then, deionized water was added to disperse and suspend them under the action of magnetic stirring. Then, the MAO powder with the corresponding mass ratio was added into the suspension and mixed, and the ultrasonic treatment with the power of 180 W was performed for 60 min. Let the beaker sit for 1 h until clear, then pour off the supernatant. After drying at 80 °C for 12 h and cooling to room temperature, the block solids in the beaker were taken out and ground to obtain the carbon -based MAO adsorbents. The samples with the AC mass percentages of 2.5 wt %, 5.0 wt %, 7.5 wt % and 10.0 wt % were labeled as AC 2.5 wt %-MAO, AC 5.0 wt %-MAO, AC 7.5 wt %-MAO, and AC 10.0 wt %-MAO, respectively. The samples with the CN, CQDs and GO mass percentages of 5.0 wt % were labeled as CN 5.0 wt %-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt %-MAO and GO 5.0 wt %-MAO, respectively. The preparation flow chart of carbon -based MAO adsorbents as shown in Fig. 1 .

The phase structure and purity of carbon -based MAO adsorbents were measured by means of DX-2007BH X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Bruker IFS 66v/S) was used to study the carbon -based MAO adsorbents in the wave-number range of 400–4000 cm−1. The charge states and compositions of carbon -based MAO adsorbents were characterized by a KRATOS X SAM 800 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The particle morphologies of carbon -based MAO adsorbents were investigated by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Nitrogen adsorption experiments of carbon -based MAO adsorbents were performed by a TriStar II 3020 surface area and porosity analyzer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of dyes, antibiotics and POPs
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption experiments of carbon -based MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of CR dye, antibiotics including TCH, OCH, CIP and NPS and TBBPA (POPs) were performed by a UV1800 UV–Visible spectrophotometer. The initial dye concentration, initial concentration of antibiotic or POPs and adsorbent content were (100, 150, 200, 250, 300 mg/L), (20, 50, 100 mg/L) and 1 g/L, respectively. During the execution of the adsorption experiment, the samples were taken every 5 min until half an hour, and the samples were taken every 30 min after half an hour. Finally, the extracted samples were centrifuged to test its absorbance. The initial absorbance of dyes, antibiotics or POPs was denoted as A0, and the absorbance at time t was denoted as At. Therefore, the adsorption percentage of carbon -based MAO adsorbents are defined as ((A0-At)/A0) × 100%.

To predict the adsorption percentage of carbon -based MAO adsorbents, back propagation (BP) neural network model was selected to train the experimental data. The absorbance curves for the adsorption of CR dye by carbon -based MAO adsorbents were used as data sets. The input of the neural network model is a 3-dimensional vector, which is the mass percentage of carbon for the carbon -based MAO adsorbents, the different carbon types and the adsorption times, respectively, and the output is the absorbance curve value, a total of 340 dimensions, corresponding to the absorbance value of 280–620 nm wavelength. The experimental data of carbon -based MAO adsorbents were divided into twenty five groups, namely, the AC 2.5 wt %-MAO, AC 5.0 wt %-MAO, AC 7.5 wt %-MAO, AC 10.0 wt %-MAO, CN 2.5 wt %-MAO, CN 5.0 wt %-MAO, CN 7.5 wt %-MAO, CN 10.0 wt %-MAO, CN 15 wt %-MAO, CN 20.0 wt %-MAO, AC, CN, MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt %-MAO and GO 5.0 wt %-MAO. In these samples, Sample AC 5.0 wt %-MAO was used to adsorb CR dye of different dye concentrations including 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 mg/L. The above mentioned samples, except for Samples AC 2.5 wt %-MAO, AC 7.5 wt %-MAO and AC 10.0 wt %-MAO, were all subjected to adsorption experiments of 200 mg/L CR dye. Sample CQDs 5.0 wt %-MAO was used to adsorb concentrations of CR dye other than 100 mg/L. Other experiments not described will be described in subsequent paragraphs. Absorbance curves corresponding to nine adsorption time points of 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 60 and 90 min. A total of 225 groups of samples was selected, and 200 groups were randomly selected for training. Twenty five inputs with the different adsorption percentages were selected for the test set. The experimental model adopts the simplest network structure with only one hidden layer, and the number of hidden layer is determined by empirical formula.
(1)
h =
x + y
+ n
Where, h is the number of nodes in the hidden layer, x is the number of nodes in the input layer, y is the number of nodes in the output layer, n is an integer between 1 and 10, so h ranges from 19 to 27. The optimal number of hidden layer nodes is 25 by using h value corresponding to the minimum mean square error value. The experimental environment and the parameter settings of the model including Tansig, Purelin, Trainlm, learning rate, target minimum error and momentum factor of computing resources are consistent with those described in literatures [33,34].

All of the produced MAO-based adsorbents were subjected to XRD characterization to detect their phase purity, crystal structure and composition. Fig. 2 (a) and (b) shows the XRD patterns of the series of AC-containing adsorbents to illustrate the differences in their MAO matrix in view of the introduction of AC component. The diffraction peaks of Samples AC 2.5 wt%-MAO and AC 5.0 wt%-MAO can well be following the crystalline phase of spinel structure MgAl2O4 with cubic phase and space group of Fd-3m (227) (JCPDS card No. 21–1152), and its diffraction peaks at 2θ of 18.9, 31.3, 36.8, 44.8, 55.7, 59.4, 65.2, 68.6, 74.1, 77.3 and 82.7° are ascribed to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), (531), (620), (533) and (444) planes of MgAl2O4, respectively. The diffraction peak of AC is not visible in the XRD pattern due to the content of AC is very small. With the increase of AC content, in addition to the diffraction peak of MgAl2O4, a weak diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.8° of AC for graphitic phase (○) can be observed in the XRD patterns of Samples AC 7.5 wt%-MAO and AC 10.0 wt%-MAO.

Fig. 2(b) shows the XRD patterns of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO. For all samples, only the diffraction peak of MAO was observed due to the content of AC, CN, CQDs or GO was less. The functional group information of carbon -based MAO adsorbents could be further confirmed by FT-IR spectra. Fig. 2(c) shows the FTIR spectra of Samples AC 2.5 wt%-MAO, AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, AC 7.5 wt%-MAO and AC 10.0 wt%-MAO. All FTIR spectra have five typical characteristic peaks around 3450, 1637, 1120, 698 and 511 cm−1, which are attributed to the O–H stretching vibration of adsorbed water, the H–O–H bending vibration of adsorbed water, the C–O functional group, the stretching vibration of Mg–O bond, and the symmetrical stretching vibration of O–Al–O bond, respectively [30]. According to the literature [32], the characteristic peak of AC may coincide with the peak of C–O functional group, which was further confirmed by the obvious change of the characteristic peak at 1120 cm−1 with the increasing of AC content. The characteristic peak at 2352 cm−1 for Samples AC 2.5 wt%-MAO, AC 7.5 wt%-MAO and AC 10.0 wt%-MAO can be ascribed to the adsorbed carbon dioxide [25].

Fig. 2(d) shows the FTIR spectra of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO. For all samples, in addition to the characteristic peaks of MAO, there are also characteristic peaks of AC, CN, CQDs or GO in carbon -based MAO adsorbents. The characteristic band in the 1200–1500 cm−1 region for the Sample CN 5.0 wt%-MAO corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–N heterocycles [35]. The characteristic peak at 1401 cm−1 for the Sample CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO can be ascribed to the CQDs [36]. The characteristic peak of Sample GO 5.0 wt%-MAO is similar to that of Sample AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, mainly because the peak of GO is also close to 1120 cm−1 [37]. To confirm that AC, CN, CQDs or GO exist in MAO, further detailed XPS characterization is needed.

To further confirm the successful preparation of carbon -based MAO adsorbents, the full -scan and high-resolution XPS spectra of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO were shown in Fig. 3 (a). From Fig. 3(a), it was evident that the characteristic peaks of Mg 1s, O 1s, C 1s, Al 2s, Al 2p and Mg 2p were observed in the full survey spectra of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO and Mg 1s, O 1s, N1s, C 1s, Al 2s, Al 2p and Mg 2p were observed in the full survey spectrum of Samples CN 5.0 wt%-MAO. The result indicates that the Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO do not contain any other impurity elements. The high-resolution Mg 1s and Al 2p spectra of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO are shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c). Table S1 shows the binding energies of Mg 1s and Al 2p for the Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO. The peaks at 1303.52–1303.89 and 74.20–74.49 eV can be assigned to the Mg2+ and Al3+ in MgAl2O4, respectively [31]. The positions of binding energies of Mg 1s and Al 2p shifted slightly depending on the type of carbon, possibly because of the special heterojunctions formed between different carbon types and MAO. Fig. 3(d) shows the high resolution N1s spectrum of Sample CN 5.0 wt%-MAO. The peaks at 394.65, 397.06, 399.48, 403.07 and 405.21 eV can be attributed to the sp2 nitrogen atoms (N2c) of the C–NC triazine ring, the aromatic cycles with the unit of N-(C)3, the hydrogen-carrying amino functional group (C–N–H), the aminogroups and the charge effect localization in heterocycles, respectively [38–40]. This finding further confirmed the presence of C3N4 in the Sample CN 5.0 wt%-MAO.

Fig. 4 shows the high-resolution C 1s spectra of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO. For all samples, a common characteristic peak may be observed at 284.81 eV, which is primarily attributed to the calibration peak of the XPS instrument itself. The characteristic peaks at 283.64, 286.24 and 288.64 eV for the Sample AC 5.0 wt%-MAO (Fig. 4(a)) can be assigned to the C–O, COH, CO in activated carbon, respectively [41]. As can be seen from Fig. 4(b), the characteristic peaks at 286.37 and 288.30 eV can be ascribed to the -C-N and -N-CN in the basic aromatic C3N4 heterocycles, respectively [42]. As shown in Fig. 4(c), two peaks are respectively located at 286.26 and 288.59 eV, which attributed to the C–OH and SP2 hybridization of CQDs, respectively [43]. The C 1s spectrum of GO 5.0 wt%-MAO (Fig. 4(d)) was able to be fitted to four components at 283.53, 284.81, 286.24 and 288.72 eV, corresponding to the CC/C–C in aromatic ring, the calibration peak of the XPS instrument, the CO functional group, and carboxyl COOH group in GO, respectively [44]. The results further confirmed the existence of different types of carbon in the respective carbon-based MAO adsorbents.

Fig. 5 shows the high-resolution O 1s spectra of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO. For the Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO (Fig. 5(a)) and CN 5.0 wt%-MAO (Fig. 5(b)), the characteristic peaks at 531.30/531.16 and 533.02/532.85 eV can be assigned to the lattice oxygen of MAO and the surface adsorbed oxygen, respectively. The O 1s spectra of Samples CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO (Fig. 5(c)) and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO (Fig. 5(d)) could be deconvoluted into three components: the lattice oxygen of MAO at 530.92/530.98 eV, the surface adsorbed oxygen at 532.36/532.30 eV and the C–O–C bond at 533.99 (O in CQDs)/533.78 (O in GO) eV [43,44]. The surface adsorbed oxygen might originate from O2 and H2O adsorbed on the surface of carbon-based MAO adsorbents and it creates oxygen vacancies [45–51]. The XPS characterization confirmed that AC, CN, CQDs or GO existed in the respective carbon-based MAO adsorbents and contained no other impurities.

SEM and TEM images have been applied to characterize the surface morphologies and microstructures of carbon -based MAO adsorbents. Fig. 6 shows the SEM images of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO. When AC and MAO were hybridized, a large number of agglomerated fine particles and rod-shaped particles with a length of about 200 nm appeared on the surface of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents. These rod-like particles are integrated into fine nanoparticles to form dense aggregates, as illustrated in Fig. 6(a). When the CN is hybridized with the MAO, a large number of fine particles appear in the samples, and the agglomeration phenomenon is evident as shown in Fig. 6(b). From Fig. 6(c), many rod-like solids, as well as particles, are present on the surface of the CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents. These rod-like particles can be formed by some fine CQDs arranged in a regular direction. To gain insight into this conclusion, TEM observation is needed. The SEM image of GO 5.0 wt%-MAO (Fig. 6(d)) demonstrates no obvious difference of size and morphology between pure MAO [30] and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO. The different morphologies of the different carbon-based MAO adsorbents may be the main reason for the slight shift of the binding energy values of the different elements. The CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents have different morphologies and have therefore been chosen as representative to perform nitrogen adsorption experiments. The specific surface areas of CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents are 172.83 and 143.85 m2/g, respectively. The pore sizes of CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents are concentrated in the micropores and mesoporous regions, suggesting that the effect of the adsorption of Samples CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO, and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO on the pore filling cannot be ignored as seen in Fig. S1.

To further observe the detailed morphology of Sample CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO, TEM characterization was performed. Fig. 7 (a) shows the TEM image of Sample CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO. It can be seen from Fig. 7 (a) that the CQDs 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents are mainly composed of fine nanoparticles and some rod-shaped particles connected by fine particles. This result is consistent with the SEM observation. The HRTEM image of Sample CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO as shown in Fig. 7(b). The HRTEM results suggested that the CQDs and MAO were dominantly exposed {002}, {400} and {311} facets, respectively. The carbon particles stick to each other, making them linked to rod-like structures. Moreover, the BF-TEM and elemental mapping images (Fig. 7(c)–7(h)) revealed that the elements such as C, Mg, Al, and O were uniformly spread on the surface of Sample CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO, which suggested that the CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents were uniformly dispersed on the carrier surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced adsorption performances for the adsorption of CR dye
Effect of different AC contents on adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para: To gain insight into the adsorption capacity of different carbon-based MAO adsorbents, CR dye was selected as the target dye for the adsorption experiments. The adsorption percentages of Samples MAO, AC 2.5 wt%-MAO, AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, AC 7.5 wt%-MAO and AC 10.0 wt%-MAO for the adsorption of CR dye as shown in Fig. S2. The adsorption percentages of Samples MAO, AC 2.5 wt%-MAO, AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, AC 7.5 wt%-MAO and AC 10.0 wt%-MAO are 58.2%, 77.6%, 89.7%, 69.1% and 65.2%, respectively. The results showed that the Sample AC 5.0 wt%-MAO had the highest adsorption percentage for the adsorption of CR dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different types of carbon on adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 (a) shows the adsorption percentages of carbon-based MAO adsorbents. The adsorption percentages of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO are 89.7%, 81.6%, 80.2% and 47.3%, respectively. Compared with above adsorbents, AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents showed the highest adsorption capacity, while GO 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents showed the worst adsorption capacity. This conclusion can also be confirmed by the non-linear pseudo-first order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO) models as shown in Fig. S3. The best-fitted model for the adsorption of CR dye on AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents was PFO. The AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents showed high equilibrium adsorption capacity for CR, ca. 173.5497, 157.1717, 170.3706 and 106.6625 mg/gcatalyst, along with the lowest K1 value, about 0.22497, 0.22259, 0.22526 and 0.33505 g/mg/min for linear PFO model, respectively, while the largest K2 value, about 0.00218, 0.00242, 0.00226 and 0.00596 g/mg/min for non-linear PFO model, respectively. Compared with other adsorbents, the AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents exhibits high adsorption capacity for the adsorption of CR dye [52–54]. Generally, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent is affected by pH value, adsorbent content, dye concentration and temperature. Therefore, the study of the influence of these parameters on the adsorption capacity of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents can serve as experimental guidance for the study of the adsorption capacity of other adsorbents. Simultaneously, the CQDs 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents showed a higher adsorption percentage than GO 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents, which may be related to the higher specific surface area of CQDs 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different pH values on adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8(b) shows the adsorption percentages of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO for the adsorption of CR dye at different pH values (CCR = 200 mg/L, CAC 5.0 wt%-MAO = 1 g/L, At = 90 min, T = 287 K). As the pH increased, the adsorption capacity of the AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents decreased. The point of zero charge (pHPZC) value of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO as shown in Fig. 8(c). The pHPZC value of Sample AC 5.0 wt%-MAO is 7.58, obviously smaller than pure MAO [30]. The result confirms that the Sample AC 5.0 wt%-MAO is positively charged in aqueous medium at pHsolution < 7.58 and negatively charged at pHsolution > 7.58. Although the pHpzc of AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents is 7.58, its adsorption capacity is relatively high at pH = 9. At pH < 9, there is a large number of OH− ions which retard the ionization of SO3 2− ion of CR dye which decreases the adsorption ability of AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents, so adsorption at acidic medium for the AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents is more preferable. At pH > 9, the dramatic decrease in the adsorption capacity of AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents may be due to the long-range repulsive interaction between the negative charged surface of AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents and anionic of CR dye. Simultaneously, the removal of CR dye onto AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents could be confirmed to be a combination of chemical and physical adsorption mechanisms due to the adsorption capacity of AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents is almost constant in the pH range of 3–9 [55]. When the pH value of the reaction solution was not adjusted, the measured pH value was 7.8, and the adsorption percentage of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents was 89.7%. Accordingly, pH = 7.8 for the CR dye was selected as the appropriate pH value for subsequent experimental studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different dye concentrations on adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8(d) shows the adsorption percentages of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO for the adsorption of CR dye at different dye concentrations (pH = 7.8, CAC 5.0 wt%-MAO = 1 g/L, At = 90 min, T = 287 K). The adsorption percentage of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents decreased with the increasing of dye concentration. This decrease is primarily due to a decrease of active functional sites on the adsorbent surface due to increased dye concentration or thermal mobility depletion [56]. After comprehensive consideration, it was found that the dye concentration of 200 mg/L was found to be the best concentration in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different adsorbent contents on adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8(e) shows the adsorption percentages of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO for the adsorption of CR dye at different adsorbent contents (pH = 7.8, CCR = 200 mg/L, At = 90 min, T = 287 K). Contrary to the variation trend of variation in dye concentration, the adsorption percentage of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents increased with the increase in adsorbent content. The results indicated that the adsorption percentage for CR dye was from 53.6 to 97.3% with the increase of the content for the AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents from 0.5 to 2 g. This phenomenon may be because of the presence of higher free active functional sites relative to the content of solute present in the reaction solution [57]. Compared with the adsorption percentage of 1 g/L adsorbent, the increase of adsorbent content of 2 g/L was not obvious, so 1 g/L was chosen as the best adsorbent content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different temperatures on adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8(f) shows the adsorption percentages of Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO for the adsorption of CR dye at different temperatures (pH = 7.8, CCR = 200 mg/L, CAC 5.0 wt%-MAO = 1 g/L, At = 90 min). The relevant parameters can be calculated by formulas (S5)-(S7). With the increase of temperature, the adsorption percentage of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents first increased and then decreased. The change in adsorption percentage of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents with the increasing temperature can be ascribed to the adsorption process is an endothermic nature. The adsorption percentage of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents decrease with the increasing of reaction temperature suggests that the adsorption process for the adsorption of CR dye occurs through physical interactions includes van der Waals forces, dipole bond forces and hydrogen bonding (Fig. S4) [58].

Adsorption isotherm models provide valuable predictive data under various parameters and experimental conditions for scientific researchers to estimate the adsorption capacity, which can be used for insight, design and optimization of adsorption process [59]. Different adsorption isotherm models including Langmuir, Tempkin, Koble-Corrigan, Toth and Freundlich models are often used to evaluate the adsorption capacity of adsorbents, so as to obtain the best prediction model. In all adsorption isotherm models, qe and Ce represent the adsorption capacity of adsorbent (mg/g) and the concentration of adsorbate (mg/L) at equilibrium, respectively.

Based on equations (S8)-(S12), Fig. 9 shows the adsorption isotherms to CR dye by Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO. The correlation parameters for the Samples AC 5.0 wt%-MAO, CN 5.0 wt%-MAO, CQDs 5.0 wt%-MAO and GO 5.0 wt%-MAO to adsorption CR dye are given in Table 1 . In the fitting curve parameters of Langmuir model, it can be seen that the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of carbon-based MAO adsorbents is AC 5.0 wt% - MAO > CQDs 5.0 wt% - MAO > CN 5.0 wt% - MAO > GO 5.0 wt% - MAO. According to the correlation coefficient R2, the Koble Corrigan model of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO and GO 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents have the best fit with the experimental data, and the Langmuir model of CQDs 5.0 wt% - MAO and CN 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents have the best fit with the experimental data. The value of parameter (n) of the Koble Corrigan model in AC 5.0 wt% - MAO and GO 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents is more than or equal to 1, indicating that the Koble Corrigan model is close to the Langmuir model and reliable [60]. As a result, the adsorption of carbon-based MAO adsorbent is primarily monolayer adsorption. Carbon-based MAO adsorbents mainly adsorb Na ion and SO3 2− in CR dye, which may show monolayer adsorption. Rather, the Temkin model is more used to describe strong intermolecular interactions between adsorbate and adsorbent, such as electrostatic interactions or ion exchange interactions. The AC 5.0 wt% - MAO, CN 5.0 wt% - MAO and CQDs 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents can also well conform to the Temkin model, but their adsorption energy (bT) is below 4.2 kJ/mol, which belongs to physical adsorption [61]. The intermolecular force between the adsorbent and Na ion and other adsorbates plays very important role in the adsorption process. This conclusion is consistent with the thermodynamic study of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents. Similarly, the parameter β1 in the Toth model is closer to 1, indicating that the adsorbent is better adapted to the Langmuir model.

Based on the BP neural network model, the adsorption percentages of CR dye adsorbed by different carbon-based MAO adsorbents were predicted, as shown in Table 2 . First, the resulting experimental data were used for training to produce a prediction model that met the error requirements. Then, the properties of other carbon-based MAO adsorbents which have not performed adsorption experiments have been predicted. From the perspective of MAO, AC and CN of a single component, the error in predicting the adsorption percentage of MAO, AC or CN for the adsorption of different CR dye concentrations is very large due to the lack of additional data. The table also shows that the prediction of adsorption percentage of Sample 5%CN-MAO is the most accurate due to the large amount of experimental data (5%AC-MAO and other CN-MAO samples) provided. Although the ratio of Sample 5%GO-MAO was similar to that of Sample 5%CN-MAO, the simulation predicted results could not meet the target requirements due to the lack of data on the adsorption percentage of carbon-based MAO adsorbents with different GO ratios. Therefore, a relatively perfect experimental data set is required to obtain the prediction results with high precision. Otherwise, it is difficult to get the predicted values which are highly consistent with the experimental data. It can also be seen from the theoretical predicted results that the best dye concentration of adsorbents is 200 mg/L, and the best mass percentage of carbon materials is 5%.

The premise of the application of adsorbent in industrial production is that it can be recycled. Before each cycle experiment, the adsorbent should be filtered, dried and sintered at 200 °C before the next adsorption experiment is performed. Fig. S5 shows the cyclic stability experiment of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of CR dye at pH = 7.8, CCR = 200 mg/L, At = 90 min, T = 287 K, CAC 5.0 wt%-MAO = 1 g/L. After five cycles, the adsorption percentage only decreased from 89.7% in the first experiment to 83.4%. This experimental result confirms that the AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbent is recyclable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption capacity of different drugs and POPs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the adsorption capacity of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of drugs and POPs, TCH, OCH, CIP, NPS and TBBPA were selected as the target adsorption drugs and POPs. Fig. S6(a) shows the effect of different TCH concentrations on the adsorption percentages of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents. For the different TCH concentrations, the adsorption percentage first increased and then almost remained constant with the increasing of adsorption time. Compared with CR dye, the concentration of drug adsorbed by the AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents is relatively low. With the increase of TCH concentration, the adsorption capacity of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents decreased. When the TCH concentration reaches 100 mg/L, the adsorption percentage is only about 28%. The results showed that the optimal concentration of TCH for the AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents was 20 mg/L.

Fig. S6(b) shows the adsorption percentages of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of different drugs including TCH, OCH, CIP, NPS and TBBPA. The adsorption percentages of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of TCH, OCH, CIP, NPS and TBBPA are 62.57%, 58.81%, 35.69%, 15.02% and 11.63%, respectively. The results confirmed that the AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents had selectivity for drug adsorption. The details of the adsorption mechanism will be discussed later.

The above analyses of the functional groups, charge states and nitrogen adsorption experiments of carbon -based MAO adsorbents and the kinetics, adsorption isotherm models and thermodynamics of the adsorption process suggested that the adsorption of CR, TCH, OCH, and CIP by AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents is a complicated process. According to the results of nitrogen adsorption experiments (Fig. S1), it was found that the pore size of carbon -based MAO adsorbent is between macropores and mesopores, indicating that pore filling plays an important role in the adsorption of CR dye on carbon-based MAO adsorbent. Fig. 10 shows the adsorption mechanism of carbon -based MAO adsorbents. Combined with the results of previous adsorption isotherm models, FTIR spectra before and after adsorption (Fig. S7 and Fig. S8) and adsorption thermodynamics calculations, it was found that the adsorption of CR dye by AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents was mainly physical adsorption. The amount of H+ on the surface of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents, making its surface more positively charged, while SO3 2− in CR dye was negatively charged. The AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents and SO3 2− in CR dye had a strong electrostatic attraction to promote the adsorption of CR dye by AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents (Fig. S7). Temkin model confirms that the intermolecular force between the Na ion in CR dye and AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents play a very important role in the adsorption process. Thermodynamic studies revealed that the adsorption process for the adsorption of CR dye occurs through physical interactions including van der Waals forces, dipole bond forces and hydrogen bonding [62–64]. Meanwhile, the ring structure on CR dye and the C–O functional group on AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents will have n-π interaction. It is worth noting that the different types of carbon provide different H+ concentrations for carbon-based MAO adsorbents, resulting in different adsorption capacities of carbon-based MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of CR dye (Fig. 8(a)). Based on the molecular structures of TCH, OCH, CIP, NPS and TBBPA (Fig. 10 and Fig. S9), the electrostatic interactions of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents on –CONH2 and –COOH are very obvious (Fig. S6(b)), while there is no relevant functional group in NPS and TBBPA, which may be the main reason for the poor adsorption capacity of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of NPS and TBBPA.

A simple ultrasound irradiation technology has been used to synthesize various carbon -based MAO adsorbents (AC- MAO, CQDs- MAO, CN- MAO and GO- MAO). The phase structure, functional group information, elementary composition, and microstructural of carbon -based MAO adsorbents were characterized by XRD, FTIR, XPS, SEM and TEM. The effects of several operating parameters such as different type of carbon, AC content, initial dye concentration, adsorbent dosage, adsorption time, pH value and reaction temperature were studied to obtain the adsorption properties of CR, TCH, OCH, CIP, NPS and TBBPA over different carbon-based adsorbents. The results demonstrate that the AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents performance well in the adsorption process for the removal rate of CR dye reachs 89.7% and the maximum adsorption capacity reachs 189.964 mg/g at pH = 7.8, CCR = 200 mg/L, At = 90 min, T = 287 K and CAC 5.0 wt%-MAO = 1 g/L. The adsorption process of AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents for the adsorption of CR dye follows the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, which is a spontaneous exothermic reaction and can be assigned to the synergistic effects of electrostatic interaction, intermolecular force, n-π interaction and hydrogen bonding. The AC 5.0 wt%-MAO adsorbents exhibit a high adsorption capacity for the adsorption of TCH, OCH and CIP and poor adsorption capacity for the adsorption of NPS and TBBPA due to the weak electrostatic interaction of AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents and –CONH2 or –COOH. The AC 5.0 wt% - MAO adsorbents have a remarkable influence on the adsorption of CR dye and TCH, OCH and CIP antibiotics, and has an excellent application in the treatment of dye wastewater and antibiotic pollution.
